---
title: "Design Declaration"
subtitle: "Punish or Reconcile? Target Framing, Performance Cues, and Authoritarian Legitimation through Repression"
author:
    - Daniel Kuhlen
    - Giovanna Lapresa
    - Jorge Zavala
date: today    
format:
  html:
    embed-resources: true
bibliography: ../../05_bibliography/bib.bib
---

```{r}
#| include: false

# packages
pacman::p_load(tidyverse, 
               rio,
               DeclareDesign,
               estimatr,
               scales)
```

# Theory

# Design

We want to implement a two-step vignette design. First, respondents are surveyed on pre-treatment variables (likely we can look for synergies in variables with other teams). Within our experiment, they are then divided into four equal vignettes along the dimensions *Restorative Justice* / *Punitive Justice* and *Threat Frame* / *No Threat Frame* and asked the first set of outcome questions. Following this, subsamples are assigned to a performance / no performance vignette and are asked the outcome battery again.

This design allows us to (1) test [@lachapelle2022] triadic theory on repression; and (2) advance the theory with a performance dimension [@przeworski2023]. In total, we expect the design to take up 4–5 minutes maximum. @fig-experiment_flowchart illustrates the experiment design. 

![Experiment Design](../../_output/figures/flowchart_randomization.png){#fig-experiment_flowchart}

## Design Declaration

The **model** assumes additive effects for the Stage 1 treatments (justice and threat frames) on support for repressive policy (0–10 scale), but allows the Stage 2 performance effect to vary across Stage 1 cells—reflecting our expectation that the performance vignette operates differently depending on the prior framing. Baseline parameters (population mean μ = 5, standard deviation σ = 2) are somewhat arbitrary at this stage.

 The **inquiry** consists of two marginal ATEs for the Stage 1 factors (estimated from Y1) and four conditional ATEs for the performance treatment, one per Stage 1 cell (estimated from Y2).
 
 The **data strategy** mirrors the two-stage complete random assignment depicted in @fig-experiment_flowchart: justice and threat frames are assigned to four cells of 500, outcomes are measured (Y1), and the performance treatment is then assigned within each cell to sub-groups of 250, after which outcomes are measured again (Y2).
 
The **answer strategy** uses heteroskedasticity-robust OLS (HC2 standard errors), with the conditional performance effects estimated by subsetting Y2 to each Stage 1 cell.

```{r}
#| label: design-declaration

# set seed
set.seed(030)

# wrap the full design in a function so parameters can be varied later (e.g. for power analysis)
declare_repression_design <- function(

  # total number of survey respondents
  N              = 2000,
  # expected average repression support score in the population (0–10 scale)
  mu             = 4,
  # population standard deviation on the outcome
  sigma          = 2,
  # how much the justice frame shifts repression support on average
  # weakest of the three treatments (cohen's d ≈ 0.40) (cohens d = tau / sigma)
  tau_justice    = 0.8,
  # how much the threat frame shifts repression support on average
  # second strongest treatment (cohen's d ≈ 0.60)
  tau_threat     = 1.2,
  # how much the performance vignette shifts support, estimated separately per stage 1 group
  # kept small as a conservative placeholder (cohen's d ≈ 0.15) — update when priors are available
  tau_perf_j0_t0 = 0.3,  # restorative justice × no threat
  tau_perf_j1_t0 = 0.3,  # punitive justice    × no threat
  tau_perf_j0_t1 = 0.3,  # restorative justice × threat
  tau_perf_j1_t1 = 0.3   # punitive justice    × threat
) {

  # m: model ------------------------------------------------------------------
  # describe the population and the process that generates outcomes

  model <- declare_model(
    N = N,
    # each respondent gets a personal noise term
    U = rnorm(N, 0, sigma),

    # stage 1 outcome: repression support after reading the justice and threat vignettes
    # we define four hypothetical scores per respondent — one for each justice × threat combination
    potential_outcomes(
      Y1 ~ mu + tau_justice * Z_justice + tau_threat * Z_threat + U,
      conditions = list(Z_justice = c(0, 1), Z_threat = c(0, 1))
    ),

    # stage 2 outcome: repression support after also seeing (or not seeing) the performance vignette
    # now eight hypothetical scores — one per justice × threat × performance combination
    # the performance shift is allowed to differ across stage 1 groups (interaction structure)
    potential_outcomes(
      Y2 ~ mu + tau_justice * Z_justice + tau_threat * Z_threat +
           (tau_perf_j0_t0 * (1 - Z_justice) * (1 - Z_threat) +
            tau_perf_j1_t0 * Z_justice        * (1 - Z_threat) +
            tau_perf_j0_t1 * (1 - Z_justice)  * Z_threat       +
            tau_perf_j1_t1 * Z_justice         * Z_threat) * Z_performance + U,
      conditions = list(
        Z_justice     = c(0, 1),
        Z_threat      = c(0, 1),
        Z_performance = c(0, 1)
      )
    )
  )

  # i: inquiry ----------------------------------------------------------------
  # define the causal quantities we actually want to know

  # average effect of the justice frame — averaged across both threat conditions
  # answers: does it matter whether policy is restorative vs. punitive?
  inquiry_justice <- declare_inquiry(
    ATE_Justice = mean(
      (Y1_Z_justice_1_Z_threat_0 + Y1_Z_justice_1_Z_threat_1) / 2 -
      (Y1_Z_justice_0_Z_threat_0 + Y1_Z_justice_0_Z_threat_1) / 2
    )
  )

  # average effect of the threat frame — averaged across both justice conditions
  # answers: does target framing as a threat increase repression support?
  inquiry_threat <- declare_inquiry(
    ATE_Threat = mean(
      (Y1_Z_justice_0_Z_threat_1 + Y1_Z_justice_1_Z_threat_1) / 2 -
      (Y1_Z_justice_0_Z_threat_0 + Y1_Z_justice_1_Z_threat_0) / 2
    )
  )

  # effect of the performance vignette within each of the four stage 1 groups
  # estimated separately because the theory expects the performance update to work
  # differently depending on which justice and threat frame the respondent already saw
  inquiry_perf_j0_t0 <- declare_inquiry(
    ATE_Perf_Restorative_NoThreat = mean(
      Y2_Z_justice_0_Z_threat_0_Z_performance_1 -
      Y2_Z_justice_0_Z_threat_0_Z_performance_0
    )
  )

  inquiry_perf_j1_t0 <- declare_inquiry(
    ATE_Perf_Punitive_NoThreat = mean(
      Y2_Z_justice_1_Z_threat_0_Z_performance_1 -
      Y2_Z_justice_1_Z_threat_0_Z_performance_0
    )
  )

  inquiry_perf_j0_t1 <- declare_inquiry(
    ATE_Perf_Restorative_Threat = mean(
      Y2_Z_justice_0_Z_threat_1_Z_performance_1 -
      Y2_Z_justice_0_Z_threat_1_Z_performance_0
    )
  )

  inquiry_perf_j1_t1 <- declare_inquiry(
    ATE_Perf_Punitive_Threat = mean(
      Y2_Z_justice_1_Z_threat_1_Z_performance_1 -
      Y2_Z_justice_1_Z_threat_1_Z_performance_0
    )
  )

  # d: data strategy ----------------------------------------------------------
  # mirror the two-stage randomization from the experiment

  # stage 1: randomly split respondents into punitive vs. restorative justice (50/50)
  assign_justice <- declare_assignment(Z_justice     = complete_ra(N, prob = 0.5))
  # stage 1: independently assign half to see the threat frame
  assign_threat  <- declare_assignment(Z_threat      = complete_ra(N, prob = 0.5))
  # record repression support after respondents read their stage 1 vignette combination
  measure_Y1     <- declare_measurement(Y1 = reveal_outcomes(Y1 ~ Z_justice + Z_threat))

  # stage 2: within each stage 1 group, randomly assign half to the performance vignette
  assign_performance <- declare_assignment(Z_performance = complete_ra(N, prob = 0.5))
  # record repression support again after the performance vignette
  measure_Y2         <- declare_measurement(
    Y2 = reveal_outcomes(Y2 ~ Z_justice + Z_threat + Z_performance)
  )

  # a: answer strategy --------------------------------------------------------
  # estimate each effect with ols; robust standard errors guard against unequal variance across groups

  # justice frame effect: compare average y1 between punitive and restorative justice groups
  estimator_justice <- declare_estimator(
    Y1 ~ Z_justice,
    .method = lm_robust, term = "Z_justice",
    inquiry = "ATE_Justice", label = "Justice Frame"
  )

  # threat frame effect: compare average y1 between threat and no-threat groups
  estimator_threat <- declare_estimator(
    Y1 ~ Z_threat,
    .method = lm_robust, term = "Z_threat",
    inquiry = "ATE_Threat", label = "Threat Frame"
  )

  # performance vignette effects: run a separate regression within each stage 1 group
  # subsetting ensures we only compare respondents who received the same prior framing
  estimator_perf_j0_t0 <- declare_estimator(
    Y2 ~ Z_performance, .method = lm_robust,
    subset = Z_justice == 0 & Z_threat == 0,
    term = "Z_performance",
    inquiry = "ATE_Perf_Restorative_NoThreat", label = "Perf: Restorative, No Threat"
  )

  estimator_perf_j1_t0 <- declare_estimator(
    Y2 ~ Z_performance, .method = lm_robust,
    subset = Z_justice == 1 & Z_threat == 0,
    term = "Z_performance",
    inquiry = "ATE_Perf_Punitive_NoThreat", label = "Perf: Punitive, No Threat"
  )

  estimator_perf_j0_t1 <- declare_estimator(
    Y2 ~ Z_performance, .method = lm_robust,
    subset = Z_justice == 0 & Z_threat == 1,
    term = "Z_performance",
    inquiry = "ATE_Perf_Restorative_Threat", label = "Perf: Restorative, Threat"
  )

  estimator_perf_j1_t1 <- declare_estimator(
    Y2 ~ Z_performance, .method = lm_robust,
    subset = Z_justice == 1 & Z_threat == 1,
    term = "Z_performance",
    inquiry = "ATE_Perf_Punitive_Threat", label = "Perf: Punitive, Threat"
  )

  # chain all mida components together in the order they occur in the study
  model +
    inquiry_justice + inquiry_threat +
    inquiry_perf_j0_t0 + inquiry_perf_j1_t0 + inquiry_perf_j0_t1 + inquiry_perf_j1_t1 +
    assign_justice  + assign_threat  + measure_Y1 +
    assign_performance + measure_Y2 +
    estimator_justice + estimator_threat +
    estimator_perf_j0_t0 + estimator_perf_j1_t0 + estimator_perf_j0_t1 + estimator_perf_j1_t1
}

# instantiate the design with default parameters
base_design <- declare_repression_design()

# run 1,000 simulated studies and compute key diagnosands (bias, power, coverage, rmse)
diagnosis <- diagnose_design(base_design, sims = 2500)

# display results as a formatted table
diagnosis$diagnosands_df |>
  select(
    Treatment  = estimator,
    `True ATE` = mean_estimand,
    Estimate   = mean_estimate,
    Bias       = bias,
    SD         = sd_estimate,
    RMSE       = rmse,
    Power      = power,
    Coverage   = coverage
  ) |>
  mutate(across(where(is.numeric), \(x) round(x, 3))) |>
  knitr::kable(
    caption = "Design diagnosis (1,000 simulations, τ_justice = 0.8, τ_threat = 1.2, τ_performance = 0.3, N = 2,000)",
    align   = c("l", rep("r", 7))
  )
```

Interpretation: Diagnosands look good for stage 1 and bad for stage 2. Substantively we are really interested how this performance-update changes the attitudes. This is the theoretical contribution to the triadic model of repression.

## Required Effect Size by Sample Size

The diagnosis table shows that stage 1 (justice and threat frames) is well-powered at N = 2,000, while stage 2 (performance vignette) is severely underpowered under conservative effect size assumptions. This motivates the question: for a given sample size, how large does the true performance effect need to be to reach 80% power? Because each stage 1 cell receives N/4 respondents and the performance treatment splits those 50/50, the effective per-arm N for each conditional performance estimate is only N/8. @fig-tau-power traces how power varies jointly across effect sizes and total sample sizes.

```{r}
#| label: tau-power-sim
#| cache: true

# helper function: a stripped-down single-cell design for the performance treatment
declare_cell_design <- function(N_cell, mu = 4, sigma = 2, tau_performance = 0.3) {

  # same logic as in the main design: baseline + performance shift + individual noise
  # but now we only have one binary treatment (performance yes/no)
  model <- declare_model(
    N = N_cell,
    U = rnorm(N_cell, 0, sigma),
    potential_outcomes(
      Y ~ mu + tau_performance * Z_performance + U,
      conditions = list(Z_performance = c(0, 1))
    )
  )

  # the quantity we want: average difference in support between performance and no-performance
  inquiry    <- declare_inquiry(ATE_Performance = mean(Y_Z_performance_1 - Y_Z_performance_0))
  # randomly assign half the cell to see the performance vignette
  assignment <- declare_assignment(Z_performance = complete_ra(N_cell, prob = 0.5))
  # observe the outcome for the assigned condition
  measurement <- declare_measurement(Y = reveal_outcomes(Y ~ Z_performance))
  # estimate the effect with robust ols
  estimator  <- declare_estimator(
    Y ~ Z_performance,
    .method = lm_robust, term = "Z_performance",
    inquiry = "ATE_Performance", label = "Performance"
  )

  model + inquiry + assignment + measurement + estimator
}

# define the range of effect sizes (tau) and total sample sizes to explore
# tau runs from 0.1 (tiny, d = 0.05) to 2.0 (very large, d = 1.0) in steps of 0.1
# n_totals are the total study sizes; each stage 1 cell gets n_total / 4 respondents,
# and the performance treatment splits those further 50/50 → n_total / 8 per arm
tau_vals <- seq(0.1, 2.0, by = 0.1)
n_totals <- c(1000, 1500, 2000, 2500, 3000)

# create one row per tau × n combination — 20 taus × 5 sample sizes = 100 designs
tau_n_grid <- expand.grid(tau = tau_vals, N_total = n_totals)

# instantiate a separate cell design for each row in the grid
# each design uses n_total / 4 as the within-cell n (one stage 1 group)
# and the corresponding tau as the assumed performance effect
designs_tau_n <- setNames(
  Map(
    function(tau, N_total)
      declare_cell_design(N_cell = N_total / 4, tau_performance = tau),
    tau_n_grid$tau, tau_n_grid$N_total
  ),
  # give each design a unique name so we can match results back to the grid later
  paste0("tau=", tau_n_grid$tau, ";N=", tau_n_grid$N_total)
)

# run 500 simulated experiments per design and record statistical power
diagnoses_tau_n <- diagnose_design(designs_tau_n, sims = 500)
```

```{r}
#| label: fig-tau-power
#| fig-cap: "Statistical power for the performance treatment within a single Stage 1 cell as a function of effect size (τ) and total sample size. Per-arm N = N_total / 8. The dashed line marks the 80% power threshold; σ = 2; 500 simulations per design."

# rebuild the lookup table with the same naming convention used for designs_tau_n
# so we can join simulation results back to their tau and n values for plotting
tau_n_lookup <- tau_n_grid |>
  mutate(design = paste0("tau=", tau, ";N=", N_total))

diagnoses_tau_n$diagnosands_df |>
  # attach tau and n_total columns to the simulation results
  left_join(tau_n_lookup, by = "design") |>
  # x-axis: effect size; y-axis: power; one coloured line per total sample size
  ggplot(aes(x = tau, y = power,
             color    = factor(N_total, labels = scales::comma(n_totals)),
             group    = factor(N_total))) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 0.8) +
  geom_point(size = 1.8) +
  # horizontal reference line at 80% — the conventional minimum acceptable power
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0.80, linetype = "dashed", color = "gray40", linewidth = 0.5) +
  annotate("text", x = 0.1, y = 0.83, label = "80% threshold",
           hjust = 0, size = 3.2, color = "gray40") +
  scale_color_brewer(palette = "Set2", name = "Total N") +
  # show power as a percentage and effect size in raw scale points
  scale_y_continuous(limits = c(0, 1), labels = percent_format(accuracy = 1)) +
  labs(x = "Effect Size (τ, scale points)", y = "Power") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12) +
  theme(legend.position = "right")
```
